flashcards.front: What is a file in the context of operating systems?
flashcards.back: A file is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage. It represents a logical I/O unit that provides a way to organize and access data persistently on storage devices.

flashcards.front: What is the file identifier and why is it important?
flashcards.back: The file identifier is a unique number (inode number in Unix) assigned by the OS to each file. It is used internally by the system to reference files, allowing multiple files to have the same name in different directories.

1) File name 2) File identifier (inode number) 3) File type 4) Location (pointer to file's address) 5) Size (current file length) 6) Protection information 7) Timestamps (creation, modification, access)

flashcards.front: What is sequential file access method?
flashcards.back: Sequential access reads/writes records one after another in order, starting from the beginning. To access a specific record, you must read all preceding records. Ideal for magnetic tape and sequential processing.

flashcards.front: What is direct (random) file access method?
flashcards.back: Direct access allows reading/writing to any record directly by specifying its record number or address, without having to read preceding records. Uses the formula: address = base + (record_number × record_size).

flashcards.front: What is indexed file access method?
flashcards.back: Indexed access uses an index file (like a book index) containing pointers to data blocks. The system searches the index first, then directly accesses the data block. Provides faster random access for large files.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between file open and file close operations?
flashcards.back: Open creates a file descriptor/handle and checks permissions, loads file attributes into memory, sets file position to beginning. Close releases the descriptor, flushes buffers, updates metadata, and frees system resources.

flashcards.front: What is a file descriptor in OS?
flashcards.back: A file descriptor is a small non-negative integer that uniquely identifies an open file for a process. It is returned by the open() system call and used in subsequent read(), write(), and close() operations.

flashcards.front: Explain the purpose of the file control block (FCB).
flashcards.back: FCB is a data structure maintained by the OS that contains all information about a file: name, location, size, protection, timestamps, current pointer position, and open mode. It is loaded into memory when file is opened.

flashcards.front: What is file buffering and why is it used?
flashcards.back: Buffering stores data temporarily in memory buffers before writing to disk or after reading from disk. It reduces the number of I/O operations by grouping small writes/reads, improving performance significantly.

flashcards.front: What are the three timestamps associated with a file?
flashcards.back: 1) Creation time - when file was first created 2) Modification time - when file content was last modified 3) Access time - when file was last read or written. These are stored in the inode/metadata.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation in file storage?
flashcards.back: Internal fragmentation occurs when allocated storage space is larger than file size (wasted space within allocated blocks). External fragmentation occurs when free storage is scattered in small non-contiguous pieces, preventing allocation of large files.

flashcards.front: What is file locking and why is it used?
flashcards.back: File locking is a mechanism to prevent concurrent access conflicts. It ensures that only one process can modify a file at a time (exclusive lock) or multiple processes can read but not write (shared lock).

flashcards.front: What is an inode in Unix/Linux file systems?
flashcards.back: Inode (index node) is a data structure that stores metadata for a file: file permissions, owner info, timestamps, size, block pointers, link count, and file type. Each file has a unique inode number.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hard links and soft (symbolic) links?
flashcards.back: Hard link is multiple directory entries pointing to the same inode; they share the same file data. Soft/symbolic link is a special file containing path to another file; it can cross filesystem boundaries and can link to directories.
